Olist Marketplace · Customer Experience Diagnostic

Two problems, not one: why Olist customers leave 1-star reviews

A Tier A diagnostic for the VP of Marketplace Operations — where a fixed quality budget should go over the next four quarters.

Brazilian E-Commerce (Olist) · 99,441 orders · Sep 2016 – Oct 2018 · observational, descriptive + predictive

Situation. Between Sep 2016 and Oct 2018, Olist grew to 99,441 orders across 27 states and thousands of sellers, with roughly three-quarters of reviews at 4–5 stars.

Complication. Yet 12.8% of delivered orders draw a 1–2 star review — and the usual scapegoat, late delivery, touches only 6.8% of orders. Something else is producing most of the dissatisfaction, and it is not obvious what.

Resolution. There are two distinct problems with two different owners.

Customer dissatisfaction has two separable causes: a severe but narrow delivery-execution failure — late orders are 6.7× more likely to draw a 1–2 star review, and lateness is concentrated in the worst 20% of sellers — and a larger, quieter product-experience failure that arrives perfectly on time and accounts for 68% of all detractors.
  1. Delivery is the sharpest lever, but a narrow one. A late order is 6.7× more likely to end in a 1–2 star review (62% vs 9%), and the effect scales with every day late — yet lateness reaches only 6.8% of orders.
  2. The lateness that exists is concentrated, so the fix is targeted. The worst 20% of sellers carry 69% of all late deliveries (Gini 0.66); 710 avoidable late orders trace to just 50 sellers, net of the distance they ship.
  3. Most dissatisfaction arrives on time — and customers say it is the product. 68% of detractors got their order on time; among them the complaint shifts from delivery to the product itself (69% name product vs 60% delivery) — invisible to every operational metric.
Recommendation Split the quality budget by owner. (1) Operations — deploy a seller-quality programme against the ~50 distance-adjusted worst sellers (11% of all late orders) and recalibrate the delivery promise, which currently runs 12 days early yet still misses; target Q1–Q2. (2) Product & marketplace — open a listing-accuracy and returns workstream on the on-time detractor population, starting with the worst categories; target Q2. Do not spend on payment/checkout UX — it is statistically flat.
6.7×
higher detractor rate when an order is late
68%
of detractors were delivered on time
69%
of late deliveries come from the worst 20% of sellers
12.8%
of delivered orders draw a 1–2 star review
What this analysis is — and is not. This is an observational, diagnostic study: it establishes what associates with dissatisfaction, not what causes it. There was no experiment, so recommendations name where to act, not a guaranteed effect size. Profitability, marketing ROI and demographics are out of scope (no cost or channel data). Star ratings understate dissatisfaction — 1.4% of commented 5-star reviews carry negative text — so every rate here is a floor.

Part I · Diagnostic — what happened

1 · Late delivery is the sharpest lever on satisfaction

The rating distribution is a cliff, not a slope: customers are delighted or furious. The clearest single driver of the furious end is whether the order arrived on time.

So WhatA late order is 6.7× more likely to draw a detractor review (62% vs 9%). This is the single largest contrast in the data — delivery execution is where operations budget belongs.
So WhatDissatisfaction scales with how late, not merely whether late — the risk climbs monotonically and saturates near 80% beyond two weeks. Arriving early buys nothing.

2 · The lateness that exists is concentrated in a few sellers

Because lateness is not spread evenly across the marketplace, the operational fix is targeting, not a blanket carrier renegotiation.

So WhatThe worst 20% of sellers carry 69% of late orders (Gini 0.66). A marketplace-wide fix would overspend on sellers already performing; a targeted one addresses most of the problem.
So WhatThe promise is padded — the median order lands 12 days early — yet misses still happen, and they blow through an already-generous buffer. Tightening the promise on reliable routes is an untapped experience lever.

3 · Most dissatisfaction arrives on time — and it is about the product

The finding that reframes the brief. If delivery were the whole story, on-time detractors would be noise. They are the majority.

So What68% of all detractors — 8,289 orders — received their parcel on time. Lateness, for all its severity, explains only a minority of dissatisfaction, and no tabular field explains the rest well.
So WhatThe text separates the two populations no metric could: late detractors blame delivery (86%); on-time detractors flip to the product (69%). Two problems, two owners.

Part II · What customers say — the text as adjudicator

4 · Customers' own words confirm the split — from an independent data source

The review text is not a fifth cause; it is the instrument that adjudicates between the four. It agrees with the structured data on the primary lever, and it resolves the on-time mystery the structured data could not.

So WhatIndependently of the model, delivery is the most-named complaint theme (54% of commenting customers). Two data sources — timestamps and free text — agree on the primary lever.
So WhatThe two-owner split is also geographic: the under-served North complains more about delivery; the high-volume Southeast, where delivery is reliable, complains about the product. Target both the owner and the region.

Part III · Predictive — how far it generalises

5 · A model confirms delivery is the driver — and shows the risk is not knowable at checkout

Trained on a temporal split (orders before 2018-05-01 predict orders after), the models turn the diagnosis into a test of predictability.

So WhatPre-delivery features lift detractor PR-AUC to 0.176; adding the realised delay more than doubles it to 0.330 — the model independently confirms delivery is the dominant driver, and that most of the signal does not exist at checkout.
So WhatThe model leans hardest on seller history, distance and the promise window — exactly the drivers the hypothesis tests confirmed. Two independent methods agreeing is the strongest claim observational data allows.
An honest negative result. The order-level late-delivery model is weak (PR-AUC 0.086 vs 0.100 baseline). Per-order pre-emptive flagging is therefore not recommended — the signal for which order runs late is mostly carrier-execution noise this dataset does not observe. Seller-level risk, by contrast, is highly predictable, which is what makes the seller-targeting recommendation viable.

Appendix · Methodology

Data & lineage

Nine Olist tables (99,441 orders) joined to one order-level base table. Reviews table read as latin-1 (UTF-8 silently corrupts Portuguese accents). Items and payments aggregated to order grain before joining; row count asserted unchanged at every step. Reviews deduplicated to the latest response per order. Analysis restricted to 96,470 delivered orders for delivery/CX metrics.

Statistical tests (effect size > p-value at n≈96k)

Late↔detractor: risk ratio 6.74 (95% CI 6.55– 6.93), Cramér's V 0.40. Distance↔lateness: Cliff's δ 0.16 (small — demoted to a risk factor). Category effect ε²=0.005 (negligible). Payment friction ε²≈0 (a well-powered null — no checkout-UX lever). All p-values Benjamini-Hochberg FDR-corrected across the 7-test family.

Models & leakage control

Temporal split at 2018-05-01 (70,582 train / 25,242 test). Three leakage defences: text features barred (written simultaneously with the score); seller history computed expanding-window with a shrinkage prior; encoders fit on train folds only. Primary metric PR-AUC (accuracy excluded on a 6.8% positive class). Detractor PR-AUC: 0.100 baseline → 0.176 pre-delivery → 0.330 with realised delay.

Text analysis

40,410 Portuguese comments (median 6 tokens). LDA rejected — too short for within-document co-occurrence; NMF + an auditable, shipped aspect lexicon used instead. Corpus is selection-biased (1-star customers comment at 2.5× the 4-star rate); every share inverse-propensity reweighted. Construct validity holds (delivery complaints 1.5× higher in late orders). Sentiment used only to audit the rating, not as an output.

Fairness & ethics

The model over-scores remote regions via distance; mitigated by ranking the seller-targeting list on distance-adjusted, seller-controllable defect and anonymising seller IDs. No review text is quoted verbatim (incidental-PII rule). No demographic data exists to audit.

Limitations

Observational (no causal claim); 25 months (~2 seasonal cycles); no cost data (impact in orders, not ROI); review non-response; late-delivery non-stationary between train and test (needs monitoring if deployed); human-labelled text validation not performed — the primary next step.

Reproducible from the notebooks 01–06 in this repository · seed 42 · Source: Brazilian E-Commerce Public Dataset by Olist (Kaggle) · Report generated from reports/_key_figures_*.json — no statistic hardcoded.
